new_findings.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

variants.claim: Talking to a fan before quoting any price lets chatters learn his job/lifestyle and set a higher first PPV price ($25 vs $10) because no anchor was established early.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters ask open questions about job and hobbies — not from a script, but organically — to extract spending-power signals. Light flirting and jokes are used; sexual escalation is avoided until the fan initiates. The chatter never names a price before this rapport window closes.

variants.claim: Every DM session — whether with a new or returning subscriber — should open with a non-sexual rapport phase to qualify the fan and create emotional investment before monetization begins.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers: 5–20 messages gathering personal info (job, hobbies) with light flirting, zero sexual content. Returning subscribers: brief small talk, ask about their day, deploy inside jokes if available. Only after this rapport block does the chatter pivot toward sexting. The report section is explicitly never scripted — it must feel genuine — but everything after it is.

sources.quote: what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200
building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse
we do use scripts inside our agency, but we never have the rapport part scripted as it's really important to let the conversation run smoothly
you're also going to get a glimpse of his spending power. So for how much you can send him PPVs
First thing you want to do when a fan comes to the page is build report. This is five to 20 messages depending on the fan and the communication
The report part is never scripted because it has to feel genuine, but everything after that is scripted

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but no chatting scripts or data-capture SOP (§8 OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pair Palm's fan CRM with a scripted data-collection intake and a build-up PPV ladder for the in-house/AI chat build.

new_findings.topic: Creator quality filter — work ethic over aesthetics
new_findings.title: Prioritize creator work ethic over follower count or physical appearance when signing

variants.claim: A less conventionally attractive or lower-follower creator with strong work ethic will generate more revenue for the agency than a high-follower or high-appearance creator who is lazy.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly tells agency owners to drop creators who are inactive or unwilling to produce content, and to instead seek out creators who are reliable and motivated even if they lack a large existing audience or a '9/10' appearance. Frames this as a simple resource-allocation decision.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

sources.quote: you can work for somebody who maybe isn't 9 out of ten or maybe doesn't have 100K followers or whatever but she has the work ethic and she's always there that's who you're going to make a lot of money with
get rid simple bro there's so many other clients out there don't waste your time

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work
work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition section (§6) has no documented qualification criteria for creators. The onboarding form captures name, email, and commission % but no work-ethic or output-commitment signals. Palm likely selects creators informally today.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a work-ethic qualification gate to the creator onboarding wizard (§6): require a brief voice memo or video response where the creator describes their current content output cadence and confirms a minimum posting commitment. Weight this more heavily than follower count in the signing decision. Also formalize an offboarding trigger: if a creator misses X consecutive content deadlines, the relationship is reviewed.

new_findings.title: Stop signing low-tier creators — quality floor determines revenue ceiling

variants.claim: Low-tier models produce low-tier results regardless of agency skill, so raising the minimum quality bar of creators signed is a prerequisite to scaling past $50K/month.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly rejects creators with low work ethic regardless of other factors. He qualifies inbound leads using story-driven case-study content (e.g. 'how we took a creator from 0 to 40K') that pre-filters serious creators, rather than vanity result screenshots. The content itself does the qualification work before any call.

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

variants.claim: Fulfillment problems start at acquisition — a creator who won't send content or holds unrealistic expectations was a selection failure, not a delivery failure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan runs an acquisition audit asking: where do the best creators come from (outbound, inbound, referrals, IG, switching agencies, existing creator networks)? What traits do the best creators share (existing social proof, consistent posting history, strong personality, existing audience, responsiveness, coachability, good on camera)? Which red flags are being ignored at signing? He distinguishes acquisition as a quality game vs. volume game depending on deal structure: pure volume only makes sense if selling a low-ticket course ($497) at scale with upsells; for a full-service agency the quality game dominates. He argues most agencies sign out of desperation to grow the inflow number and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later.

sources.quote: if you keep acquiring low tier models, you're going to get low tier results because you got low tier work ethic and it's not going to scale
if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it
most agencies are nowhere near picky enough. They're so desperate to sign creators that they just ignore any of the red flags and then complain about fulfillment 30 days later
fulfillment starts at acquisition. You cannot outdeliver a bad creator selection forever
acquisition is a quality game or a volume game depending on the business model, depending on the deal structure

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is outbound-DM-led with no documented quality-scoring rubric for creator tiers (§6). There is no lead-magnet content funnel that pre-qualifies creator quality before outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum creator quality rubric (work ethic signals, existing audience, content output history) and apply it as a gate before onboarding. Produce story-driven case-study content (process videos, not just stat screenshots) to attract self-selecting high-tier creators inbound.

new_findings.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

sources.quote: the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text
For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day
I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led but not run as a tracked, committed-volume system (§6 THIN); no defined daily quota or multi-account structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize Palm outbound as a committed daily-volume system (per-day DM quota, multi-account, voice/video openers, qualify-in-conversation) rather than ad-hoc DMs.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster
use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator 2–3 content verticals matched to her strengths, then iterate on viral data

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable
We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator
within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline scrapes viral inspo and promotes reels to a creator's board (§1), which is data-driven but inspo-led rather than creator-vertical-led. There is no documented step where a creator is formally assigned 2–3 fixed verticals as a strategic frame before ideation begins.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'vertical assignment' step at creator onboarding: define 2–3 content verticals per creator based on her strengths/interests, then filter inspo scraping (§1 pipeline) to those verticals first. After 6 weeks of posting, run a formal data review to codify which verticals are outperforming, and adjust the inspo source list accordingly.

new_findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

new_findings.title: Qualify creators against a checklist and disqualify on red flags

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

sources.quote: Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work
if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding captures admin fields but has no documented fit scorecard or red-flag screen (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an ideal-client checklist plus a red-flag screen to the onboarding/intake step so Palm signs for fit and retention, not just availability.

new_findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels
the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

new_findings.topic: Onboarding sequence — OF page setup before social access
new_findings.title: Structured 7-day onboarding: OF page + vault first (days 1–4), social access + content plan second (days 5–7)

variants.claim: A sequenced onboarding where the OF page is optimized and chatting infrastructure is ready before social accounts are touched reduces errors and accelerates time-to-revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Days 1–2: get OF login and Inflow access, optimize vault and OF page. Days 3–4: bio, subscription price, page optimization. Days 5–7: gain IG/TikTok/Twitter access, set up content distribution, scripts, chatting setup, and build content plan with creator. Content plan uses the 3-competitor research process (top videos → style selection → post → teach creator what to film and why).

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: Log in to the only fans in the inflow account. Optimize the vault, optimize the OF page. Then on days like seven, I want you to get into the Instagram, Tik Tok, and Twitter accounts
content strategy and create a content plan with her. So on that, I want you to do niche competitor research
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.
if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding form captures credentials and contract (§6) but there is no documented day-by-day onboarding sequence or ordering logic. The OF vault/PPV system is thin (§2) and no OF page optimization checklist exists. The social setup is mature for AI accounts (§4) but not formalized for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a real-creator onboarding SOP mirroring the AI warmup cadence structure (§4): Days 1–4 = OF access + page/vault optimization checklist; Days 5–7 = social access + content plan creation. Add this as a checklist workflow in Airtable under the Creators table, triggered on creator status change to 'onboarding.'

new_findings.title: Main + 2-4 Burner Account Structure to Grow X Presence Fast

variants.claim: Running one premium 'main' account supported by 2–4 high-volume burner accounts is the core engine for rapid X follower growth and OF revenue.
variants.what_they_do: One main account is treated as the premium asset — high quality content only, no full nudity, goal of 50–100K impressions per post. Two to four burner accounts run parallel at higher volume (3–5 posts/day vs 1–3 on main), used for trend testing, engagement farming, and follower accumulation. Once burners hit 1–5K followers, all traffic is funneled to the main via pinned tweets, bio links, and reply CTAs. Main account holds the OF link; burners never monetize directly.

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

variants.claim: Running hundreds of warmed-up Instagram accounts via cloud phones on automated schedules generates 50–200 additional subs per day per creator while insulating the main account from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency spins up 200–300 Instagram accounts on cloud-hosted real phones. Automations handle: feed scrolling, liking, Reel posting, phone-number verification — all to mimic organic human behavior and avoid suspensions. None of these satellite accounts carry the OF link in bio; they funnel only to the creator's main IG page (or to 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives only on the main page. Suspension rate drops to ~1-in-20 vs. higher rates when the OF link is in satellite bios. If main account is banned, warmed satellite accounts are handed to the creator to restart on.

sources.quote: You're going to have two to four accounts in this depending on where you're at. The account one is going to be your main account.
These are your growth engine
we set up hundreds of Instagram accounts... you can run everything off the cloud
In none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages
if we have 200 phones for one creator, and we're literally pulling one subscriber per day from each of the phones, then that's 200 subs a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm has no X/Twitter presence or strategy documented. This is a fully absent channel for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 1-main + 2-burner X account structure for each real creator. Assign a VA to run burner engagement (spam commenting, trend monitoring, retweet loop) daily. Main account managed by editor/ops. Start with one creator as a test before rolling out.

new_findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars
you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

new_findings.title: Productized fulfillment makes the creator the only variable

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

sources.quote: Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable
Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is productized (§1 MATURE) but OF vault/PPV and chat delivery are not standardized (§2 THIN, §8 OUTSOURCED); no single end-to-end fulfillment machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's productized social pipeline into a full standardized fulfillment system (page setup, welcome message, funnel, chat) so new creators plug into one machine and revenue isn't concentrated in a few creators.

new_findings.topic: Social content strategy — filler vs. vertical content split
new_findings.title: Two-content-type IG framework: 'filler' (lifestyle) keeps algorithm happy; 'vertical' (viral-engineered) drives new traffic

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

sources.quote: Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process
Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature 8-step inspo-driven pipeline that uses viral data to decide what creators film (§1), which directly parallels the competitor analysis piece here. However, Palm's posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP). The filler vs. vertical content taxonomy is not explicitly documented in Palm's system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formally adopt the filler/vertical taxonomy inside Palm's content pipeline. Tag each Asset in Airtable as 'filler' or 'vertical/traffic-driver' so editors and schedulers can enforce the 2–3/day cadence with the right mix. This codifies the cadence GAP noted in §1.

new_findings.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
new_findings.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

new_findings.title: Use passive teaser content (towel/shower clips) to make the fan initiate sexual escalation, not the chatter

variants.claim: Chatters should never directly initiate sexual conversation — instead, send an ambiguous teaser that makes sexual escalation the fan's idea, reducing friction and preserving the illusion of authenticity.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends a low-reveal teaser (e.g., model in a towel with wet hair, caption: 'just got out of the shower') with no sexual framing. The fan reads the signal and responds sexually — he has technically initiated. Alternatively, a 3-second clip of the model waving to camera with a self-deprecating caption ('my face looks so bad right now') achieves the same passive initiation. Once the fan escalates, the chatter follows his lead and layers in a second, slightly more revealing teaser before setting up the first PPV.

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation
post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8 outsourced). This is a high-value build-toward mechanic — it maps directly to the psychological structure Palm will need when scripting AI chatting (active roadmap project, §8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: In the in-house/AI chat SOP, codify 'fan-initiated escalation' as a required step between rapport and first PPV. Build a library of passive teaser content categories (shower, waving, casual home setting) and matching neutral captions. For AI chatting specifically, this mechanic is low-risk and high-leverage — the AI never has to make a sexual first move.

new_findings.title: Three chatters + reliever for seamless 24/7 shifts

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

sources.quote: Three chatters and one reliever
standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting and has no internal shift model (§8). This is a concrete staffing template for the future in-house team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 3+1 eight-hour shift model with Discord clock-in/EOS reports as the staffing blueprint when Palm builds its in-house chat team.

new_findings.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result
media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

new_findings.title: Hire for coachability and reliability, not pre-existing chatting skill

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.
Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring criteria (§8 outsourced). This philosophy should anchor Palm's future hiring rubric — it means Palm can source from a much wider talent pool and doesn't need to pay a premium for 'experienced' chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document 'coachability, reliability, honesty' as Palm's three core chatter hiring criteria. Build the scoring rubric for each screening stage around these three traits rather than chatting knowledge. This also reduces cost — experienced chatters command higher pay; trainable reliable people do not.

new_findings.title: Source chatters from onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, OFM Jobs — never Telegram

variants.claim: The three reliable hiring platforms for chatters are onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs; Telegram chatters are consistently low quality.
variants.what_they_do: Post job listings on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Explicitly avoid sourcing from Telegram groups. Also mentions some Facebook groups and other Filipino hiring sites as secondary options.

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: post a job post on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Those are like the three main hiring sites. Don't get them off of Telegram.
Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team (§8); no internal hiring process exists yet. When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr out), it has no documented sourcing channels. This is a direct gap to fill.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, establish onlinejobs.ph as the primary sourcing channel. Create a blacklist policy against Telegram-sourced chatters. Document this as the first step in the future chatter hiring SOP.

new_findings.topic: X content rules — tease vs. reveal
new_findings.title: No Full Nudity on X — Use Tease-Only Content to Pull Higher-Spending Fans

variants.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
variants.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

sources.quote: Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.
on Twitter you can get away with more not safeer workor content so AKA showing everything and the chance of that going viral is a lot higher and Twitter's full of that right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault content is a documented GAP (§2 — thin). This finding reinforces a content tiering principle: social = tease, OF = explicit. Palm's social pipeline (§1) produces top-of-funnel content but has no explicit rule documented about nudity levels per platform. This is a rule Palm should codify.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-tier rule to Palm's SOP: X/IG/TikTok = tease only (no nudity, professional quality). OF vault = exclusive explicit. Document this as a creator briefing item in onboarding and in the editor task spec so editors never post full nudity to social channels.

new_findings.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

new_findings.title: Founder should spend hours/day on the highest-leverage asset

variants.claim: Founders should reallocate large blocks of daily time to the single highest-leverage activity (brand/distribution) and treat inability to do so as a sign the business is structured wrong.
variants.what_they_do: Argues for 4+ hours/day on personal brand and says if you don't have that time, the business is set up wrong and you're stuck firefighting; pushes decentralizing operations so the founder works on leverage, not in the hamster wheel.

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: you need to be spending 4 hours a day on this. Like minimum
if you haven't got 4 hours... a day to put into your personal brand, you're doing something wrong with your business
take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10): Josh carries CEO + acquisition + strategy with no org chart or delegation plan; tech/ops concentrated on Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit founder time and protect daily blocks for the highest-leverage work (acquisition/brand), delegating firefighting — ties directly to the teammate-org initiative.

new_findings.title: Avoid low-ticket volume models: getting 2 premium clients is orders of magnitude easier than getting 50 low-ticket ones

variants.claim: Acquiring two high-paying clients requires far less effort than acquiring fifty low-paying ones, making low-ticket volume models a trap for agency owners.
variants.what_they_do: Frames the math explicitly: 50 clients at $97/month = $5,000; 4 clients at $2,500 = $10,000 with a fraction of the acquisition and support overhead. Argues that there is 'not really that much extra work' to target higher-paying clients but the upside is dramatically better.

variants.claim: Agencies stuck at $20K/mo with 8–9 small creators ($2–5K each) are less profitable and harder to scale than agencies with 3–4 creators each maximized sequentially; focus on one creator until capped, then move to the next.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly advises: sign one creator, put all focus on growing her to maximum revenue, then only take on the next creator when bandwidth permits. Barry runs 4 creators with one responsible for ~$50K alone. The agency avoids the trap of signing volume for its own sake. Paid promo layered on top of organic is used to stabilize revenue so the agency isn't 'one bad algorithm month away from losing everything.'

sources.quote: getting two clients, even at a higher price point, is orders of magnitude easier than getting 20, 30, 40, 50 lower paying clients
they have like nine creators all at like two three four five it's like just a bunch of creators
I get one model and I'll just try to max it out as much as possible and then from there when I feel ready and I have time enough to do it I'll just move on to the next one

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's revenue is creator-revenue-share-based, so the direct pricing analogy doesn't apply, but the portfolio concentration principle does. If Palm signs many small creators with low earning potential to hit volume, management overhead scales while revenue per creator stays low. No documented policy on portfolio size vs. creator quality exists (§6, §7 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a minimum viable creator revenue threshold before signing. Prioritize signing 4–8 high-earning creators over 20+ micro-earners. Document this as a founding portfolio strategy principle to guide Josh's acquisition decisions and Evan's ops planning.

new_findings.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition approach is Josh-DM-led without a documented onboarding capacity limit (§6). There is no written rule about how many creators Palm takes on relative to operational readiness (chatting outsourced §8, thin content vault §2). This principle is implicitly understood but not codified as a scaling gate.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's current operational capacity ceiling per creator tier — e.g., how many creators can be served well given current editor headcount, outsourced chat team bandwidth, and social pipeline throughput. Use this as a hard gate in the acquisition process so new signings only happen when capacity exists, preventing the quality dilution Dylan describes.

new_findings.title: Group-chat situational awareness test to screen for sales instinct before any live interview

variants.claim: A 10-question situational/emotional intelligence test sent to all shortlisted applicants in a single group chat efficiently identifies candidates with natural sales instinct without requiring individual calls.
variants.what_they_do: Create a group chat (WhatsApp or Telegram) with all applicants who passed Stage 1. Send a Google Docs with 10 situational questions designed to reveal emotional intelligence and objection-handling instinct — not chatting skill. Example: 'You own a vegan restaurant and someone asks for steak — what do you say?' The 'right' answer reframes the objection and redirects to an alternative rather than refusing. This filters out poor communicators before investing time in live interviews.

variants.claim: A live Discord call with screen-sharing — covering typing speed, English proficiency, and a live camera reveal of the candidate's physical workspace — surfaces reliability signals that asynchronous tests cannot.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 3 live assessment via Discord with screen-share: (1) words-per-minute typing test (candidates will cheat; if you can't catch it, they earned it); (2) live English proficiency test using any free online tool; (3) ask the candidate to turn their camera and film their desk/setup in real time. The desk reveal is flagged as unique and highly valuable — an organized vs. disorganized desk is treated as a proxy for how they'll manage their work. Philosophy: 'how you do one thing is how you do everything.'

sources.quote: create a group chat with all of the applicants and you're going to give them their first test... a situational awareness test
you are the owner of a vegan restaurant and someone comes in asking for real meat, for a steak. What would you tell that person?
let them hop on a Discord live call with like a shared screen... words per minute test, how fast they can type
ask them, 'Hey, quickly turn your camera around and show me your setup.' Because it shows you so much about what type of person they are.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter screening baseline (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward for the in-house chat team. Palm's current external chat team presumably handles their own quality — Palm has no visibility into how those chatters were vetted.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a Palm-specific situational awareness test (10 questions) as Stage 2 of the future chatter hiring pipeline. Frame questions around sales redirection rather than OF-specific knowledge. Use a group chat delivery mechanism to batch-process applicants efficiently.

new_findings.topic: Pre-launch network building before signing creators
new_findings.title: Build a distribution network of 10–20 partner creators before signing your first creator in a niche

variants.claim: An agency entering a new niche should develop a promotional network of at least 10–20 creators and agencies before signing any creator, so that traffic infrastructure is ready on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Before signing a single trans creator, the agency owner spends time in Telegram groups building relationships with 10–20 trans creators and several agencies that manage trans creators. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules GGs and S4S deals with this network so that as soon as a creator is onboarded, a surge of guaranteed fans (e.g. 10,000 on day one via $5K in GGs) can be deployed immediately. This inverts the usual sequence of 'sign creator, then figure out traffic.'

variants.claim: Developing a network of same-size trans creators and agencies for mutual shoutouts on OF, IG, Telegram, TikTok, and X before launch allows an agency to spike a new creator to tens of thousands of fans on day one.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner joins as many OFM Telegram groups as possible, posts availability for S4S and GG collabs, and records all contacts in a spreadsheet. Matching logic: pages of roughly equal fan count do S4S (mutual shoutout); significantly larger pages are approached for paid GGs instead. Pre-launch, the agency pre-schedules promos from the entire network so that on launch day the new creator immediately receives a surge of fans. Once internal pages grow, new creator launches are boosted by the agency's own portfolio of existing creators ('internal boosting'), eliminating the need to pay for early GGs.

sources.quote: before you sign any trans girls, I'd say start developing your network — get at least like 10–20 trans creators that you're in contact with
as soon as you launch, have all of them pre-schedule everything so you have like $5,000 in guaranteed gains coming in
you're going to gain most of your network through Telegram groups
when you onboard new creators, you pre-schedule promos from your entire network and all of your internal pages as well, boosting new pages to $100,000 in the first month

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led and reactive (§6 THIN). There is no documented pre-signing network-building step or launch-day traffic playbook. Palm typically figures out distribution after a creator is onboarded.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any new niche Palm enters (including trans if that becomes a focus), add a pre-launch checklist step: minimum N partner contacts secured in Telegram before creator onboarding begins. Document required contacts and deal commitments as a gate in the creator onboarding flow, making distribution readiness a precondition of launch rather than an afterthought.

new_findings.title: Coach creators on 4 non-negotiable quality standards: lighting, audio, background, and variety

variants.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.

variants.claim: Agencies must tell models exactly what content types they need at onboarding — not generically 'more content' — so all traffic and monetization strategies can be activated immediately.
variants.what_they_do: During the onboarding call they specify four distinct content buckets needed upfront: (1) sexting/script sets for DM selling, (2) PPV bundles and standalone videos for mass messages, (3) SFW promotional content for Reddit, Twitter, and Instagram, (4) verification posts for subreddits that require them. They also provide a weekly content schedule so the model knows what to film the following week.

sources.quote: lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage
scripts so like live content that you could sell via sexting sets in the DMS for of you also need to have ppv bundles and and videos that you can use to mass message out
providing some sort of content schedule to them on a weekly basis as to what you would likely need for that following week

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) evaluates *incoming* inspo reels for viral score but there is no documented creator-facing quality brief or production standards SOP. Quality feedback is presumably reactive via the revision workflow (Tasks table).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Content Quality Standards' PDF/Notion doc covering the four pillars above. Send it during onboarding and reference it in weekly content briefs. Add a quality-gate field to the Assets Airtable table so editors can flag substandard submissions for coaching rather than silently fixing or discarding.

new_findings.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

variants.claim: A single visual Miro board showing all employees with ratings, target revenue, timeline progress, current revenue, and per-creator ratios makes every bottleneck visible and creates a clear step-by-step operating orientation for the agency owner.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin builds and shares a Miro board with each student/client. The board contains: all team members with a performance rating next to each, target revenue goal, timeline to hit it, current revenue, and per-model revenue ratios. It is described as 'basic tracking' — not pulling granular IG KPIs — but the visual format makes bottlenecks immediately apparent. It is credited as the primary driver of Barry's jump from $80K to $100K/mo.

sources.quote: the model account manager
you built this entire like visual map of my agency that I've never seen in that way every bottleneck was just visible right there on the screen
we have every single one of your employees with a rating next to it the target revenue the progress to the timeline your current revenue your ratios for each model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.

new_findings.title: Supervised shadow → monitored practice → independent KPI ramp for new chatters

variants.claim: New chatters should follow a structured three-phase ramp: shadow existing chatters, then practice under live supervision, then work independently under KPI monitoring — rather than going live solo immediately.
variants.what_they_do: Phase 1 (first ~week): new chatter shadows and observes existing chatters in real shifts. Phase 2: chatter handles 2–3 hours of live chat themselves while a supervisor watches in real time. Phase 3: chatter works independently while a supervisor continues to monitor shifts, gives feedback, and answers questions. KPIs and quality controls are only introduced once the chatter reaches a stable independent baseline.

variants.claim: Embedding hidden checkpoints (emoji signals, mid-video questions) inside a mandatory 90-minute onboarding video filters out candidates who won't follow through — before they ever touch a live fan account.
variants.what_they_do: After passing all screening stages, selected candidates receive a 90+ minute video covering: how OnlyFans works, how the CRM (Creator Hero) works, scripts, hotkeys, and job structure. The video includes 2–3 embedded accountability checks — e.g., 'send me a cherry emoji if you watched to here' or answer specific questions after viewing. Anyone who fails to complete or respond is cut. Rationale: if a chatter won't watch a Loom on Day 1, they won't perform reliably in month 2.

sources.quote: let them watch other people, let them supervise other chatters for a couple of days for the first week. Then let them do their initial practice, let them chat for like two or three hours themselves with a supervisor watching.
send them like a 90-minute plus video explaining the entire job... include a couple of accountability checks
if someone isn't even watching a Loom video you sent them at their first day on the job, you can imagine how he's going to work in a month from now

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal ramp-up process (§8 outsourced). This structured ramp is essential for Palm's in-house chat team plan and maps well to Palm's existing supervisor/manager role structure (§10 chat_manager Clerk role exists).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this three-phase ramp as the standard onboarding track for all in-house chatters. Define the Phase 2→3 promotion trigger (e.g., supervisor scores 3 consecutive satisfactory shifts). Assign the chat_manager Clerk role as the supervisor for Phase 1–2 monitoring. Codify KPI thresholds before Phase 3 begins.

new_findings.title: Pay X Promo Pages $50–$150 per Post to Inject 500–2K Followers Fast

variants.claim: Third-party X 'promotional pages' (50K–500K followers) that post teaser/clickbait content about a creator and then get a staged reply from the creator's account are a highly cost-effective paid acquisition channel on X.
variants.what_they_do: Find accounts (50K+ followers) that post blurry/frame teaser content with clickbait captions like 'Holy shit I can't believe she did this on camera — thread.' Pay them $50–$150 per post. The creator's burner or main account then 'discovers' the post and replies with the real content plus an OF CTA (e.g., 'OMG that's me — I made it free on my OF for the next 17 people'). Identifies promo pages by: always mention 'thread' in caption, post multiple different models, 50K+ followers. Frequency: run a promo every 2–4 weeks per account that is stagnant. One promo post can yield 500–2,000 new followers per day.

variants.claim: Paying $20–$50 to boost a tweet that already hit 10K+ impressions organically compounds reach cheaply because the algorithm signals are already proven.
variants.what_they_do: Let content run organically first. If a post reaches 10K impressions without paid spend, that is the signal to boost it via Twitter's native promotion tool. Spend $20–$50 per boost. Logic: high organic engagement + paid boost = outsized reach because Twitter's algorithm already validated the content. Only boost proven winners, never cold content.

sources.quote: These are accounts with absolutely massive following, 50 to 500,000 followers that post teaser content for your models.
