I want you to DM them saying, hey, I'm looking to promote my account. What are your rates?
If a post hit 10K impressions organically, promote it. Twitter will let you boost tweets for 20 to 50 bucks, and this puts your content in front of people for more reach.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no X strategy at all (§5 GAP). Palm does use paid promotion for IG (implied in pipeline), but no equivalent X promo-page tactic exists. This is a new channel + tactic combination.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Source 3–5 verified X promo pages in each creator's niche. Negotiate a test post at $50–$100. Pre-script the creator's 'reply' post with OF CTA and urgency hook. Track follower gain and OF tracking link conversions per promo. Build a promo-page vendor list in Airtable (name, follower count, niche, rate, past results).

new_findings.title: Casino psychology: keep fans in the dopamine loop and stay in control

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

sources.quote: in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan intelligence via Fan Tracker (§9 PARTIAL) but has no chatting SOP using it (§8 OUTSOURCED); the rapport-and-psychology loop is undocumented build-toward intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Specify a rapport-and-control chatting playbook (onboarding DM, fan-note schema, no-price-talk, power-dynamic rules) for the in-house/AI chat roadmap, wired into Fan Tracker.

new_findings.title: Trend-Jack Active X Formats Daily for Guaranteed High-Impression Posts

variants.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.

sources.quote: Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is already data-driven and scrapes viral Reels from IG inspo accounts — this is exactly the same logic applied to X. Palm's system could theoretically be extended to scrape X trending formats. Currently Palm has no X monitoring in the Apify/inspo pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add X trend monitoring to the content pipeline: include 5–10 X creator accounts in the Apify scrape or add a manual daily X check as a VA task. When a trending format is identified, create an X-specific task card in Airtable so the creator can produce the asset same-day. Mirror Palm's existing inspo-promote-film loop for X.

new_findings.title: Personal/agency brand is the real acquisition constraint

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no agency/founder brand or content presence for acquisition (§6 THIN); every Josh DM starts from zero trust.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh (or a Palm-brand channel) publish consistent niche authority content so inbound trust precedes outreach, reducing reliance on cold DMs.

new_findings.title: Track the full outreach funnel and fix one stage at a time

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach funnel metrics (reply/booking/show/close) or follow-up cadence (§6 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Stand up an outbound tracker (reply, qualified, booked, showed, closed) and a multi-touch follow-up sequence, mirroring the data-driven discipline Palm already applies to content.

new_findings.title: Use weekly invoicing explicitly as a trust signal, not just a payment mechanism

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

sources.quote: always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already auto-generates weekly invoices (§7 — multi-account PDF via Puppeteer, Dropbox, Resend). However, Palm's invoicing system is described as an ops tool, not a trust/relationship tool. There is no documented practice of *explaining* the invoicing schedule during onboarding or positioning it as a transparency signal to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an 'invoicing walkthrough' step to the onboarding call checklist: show the creator the invoice format, confirm the payment cadence, and explicitly frame it as mutual accountability. This costs nothing — Palm's invoice infrastructure already exists — but closes the relationship-framing gap.

new_findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

new_findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.title: Assign each creator a hobby-based niche to give the algorithm a clear target audience

variants.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
variants.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.

sources.quote: if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on data-driven ideation from viral inspo reels but does not document a niche-assignment step per creator. The system scrapes what is already viral broadly; it does not codify the step of choosing one thematic niche per creator to anchor the algorithm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'niche selection' step to Palm's creator onboarding and content pipeline (§1, step 5 or pre-step). For each creator, define one or two content niches based on their genuine interests. Feed that niche as a filter into the Apify scrape / Inspo Sources targeting so viral reference reels are pulled from that niche's top performers, not just broadly popular content.

new_findings.title: Position the agency offer around creator outcomes (time saved, revenue growth), not the 50% split

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission model or offer framing (§7 GAP). Commission % is set per-creator at onboarding with no stated rationale or tiering. This is Palm's #1 identified gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's standard offer narrative: lead with the two pain points (fan growth, time), show a concrete growth trajectory from an existing client, and frame the 50% split as a performance-aligned partnership rather than a fee. Consider creating a one-page 'offer doc' with revenue-growth evidence that can be shared during acquisition outreach.

new_findings.title: Model Reddit captions on top-performing posts in target subreddit, then iterate own voice

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) extracts hooks and captions from viral IG reels for social content. The same intelligence layer does not exist for Reddit. No Reddit caption SOP documented (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the Airtable content pipeline to include a Reddit Caption Bank: a table of top-performing captions per subreddit, refreshed weekly, used as briefs for whoever manages the Reddit accounts. This reuses the same inspo-scrape logic Palm already has — just pointed at Reddit instead of Instagram.

new_findings.title: Run 1–30 on-brand comment baits per day per Threads account to boost engagement signals

variants.claim: Strategic comment baiting on Threads boosts engagement signals, but must look human and on-brand to avoid spam detection.
variants.what_they_do: TDM plants 1–10 comment baits per day per account (capped at 25–30 max) — posts or replies designed to provoke comments. They require the bait to be niche-relevant, human-sounding, and directly related to the original post to avoid spam flags. Volume is scaled based on page maturity.

sources.quote: most important thing with comment baiting is that it looks on brand and looks human. It has to relate to the niche that the creator is in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented engagement-baiting tactic for any platform (§5 GAP). This is a traffic/distribution gap. Palm's Telegram-routed manual posting (§5) could incorporate comment baiting as an Amin task.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a comment-baiting step to the Threads posting SOP: 1–10 baits/day for new accounts scaling to a max of 25–30 for mature accounts. Include a brief checklist (niche-relevant, human tone, linked to original post) in the Telegram task instructions sent to Amin.

new_findings.title: Segment OF creators into 4 revenue tiers to target the right clients

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented targeting criteria for which creator tier to pursue (§6 GAP). Acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no written segmentation model. This competitor has a concrete framework that tells a new agency owner exactly which creators to approach and why.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a one-page creator segmentation model for Palm: define the four revenue tiers, articulate the ROI argument for each, and codify which tier(s) Palm currently targets and why. Use this as a qualifier in outbound DM scripts so Josh — and eventually any acquisition hire — is fishing in the right pond from day one.

new_findings.title: Differentiate from automated competitors by calling clients and treating them as real people

variants.claim: At premium price points, agencies can and should offer direct human communication — calls, responsive emails, real answers — which becomes the actual differentiator against lower-priced automated competitors.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly offers phone calls, responsive emails, and real conversations with clients as part of service delivery. Frames this as only economically viable at $1,500–$2,500+/month (can't afford it at $97). Positions human accessibility as the competitive moat against SaaS/AI commodity offerings.

sources.quote: we're going to be normal human beings. That's right. We're going to call them. We're going to send them emails. We're going to answer their questions.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator relationships are managed by Josh (acquisition/strategy) but there is no documented creator success cadence, check-in frequency, or retention SOP (§9 🔲 GAP). Creators are the 'clients' in Palm's model. The risk of churn from creators feeling unsupported is real and undocumented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a creator success cadence: e.g., weekly performance update (automated from portal data), bi-weekly check-in call with Josh or Evan, monthly revenue review. Document this as Palm's retention differentiator. This directly addresses the §9 gap and reduces creator churn risk.

new_findings.topic: Payment collection method — bank swap vs invoice
new_findings.title: Two-method payment collection: bank-info swap vs end-of-period invoice

variants.claim: Agencies can either replace the model's OnlyFans bank details with their own (leaving the model's name intact) or simply invoice the model for their commission percentage after the model withdraws earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Method 1: Agency swaps the bank account number on the creator's OF account to the agency's account, leaving the account name unchanged so OF doesn't flag it; agency receives all revenue, then pays creator their cut. Method 2: Agency leaves bank info as-is, creator withdraws at month/week end, agency sends a formal invoice (date, agreed split, amount owed, payment deadline) and creator pays the agency's cut separately. Bjorn says he now uses Method 2 for nearly all models and is migrating to 100% invoicing.

sources.quote: at the end of the month you send your invoice to the model saying here's everything your account is earned for the month then here's the percentage we graded on minus that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses invoicing (§7): weekly auto-computed PDFs via Puppeteer, emailed via Resend, with commission % set at onboarding. Method 1 (bank swap) is not part of Palm's documented approach. Palm's invoicing is weekly; Bjorn describes monthly or weekly invoicing as a choice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm's existing weekly invoicing aligns with Bjorn's preferred Method 2 — no change needed on the fundamental approach. However, Palm should explicitly document WHY it uses invoicing (trust signal to creators, easier onboarding, avoids virtual-bank bans) as part of creator pitch/onboarding materials so managers can articulate it confidently during signing conversations.

new_findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

new_findings.title: Gate chatter hires on English, typing speed, and connection before training

variants.claim: Screen chatters on measurable competencies before hiring and training, since bad chatting silently destroys revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Before hiring a chatter, run an English skill test, a Wi-Fi speed test, and a words-per-minute test; only those who pass get hired and trained. Bad chatters (spelling/grammar errors, mispriced first PPVs like $4.99 or $200) are a top-7 agency-killing mistake.

sources.quote: we give them an English skill test... a Wi-Fi speed test... a words per minute test. Only once they check out on all of these, that is when you hire them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced (§8) so Palm doesn't run this screen today; it's build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team's hiring SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build-toward: when standing up the in-house chat team, adopt a pre-hire screen (English / WPM / connection) and a first-PPV pricing standard.

new_findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

consensus_risers.title: Train chatters on a fixed funnel: questions, imposition, pull-push, guilt trips

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

variants.claim: Rather than describing the video's explicit content directly (which sounds repetitive and generic), chatters sext about an unrelated fantasy the creator 'has been having,' escalate it fully, then link the PPV as what the creator made while thinking about that fantasy.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter chooses a fantasy (threesome, free use, femdom, cheating, etc.) — ideally one the subscriber has shown interest in — and opens with 'there's something I've been thinking about but I'm embarrassed to say.' They build out the fantasy in full descriptive detail, getting the sub emotionally invested. When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question
descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.
an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting with no internal SOP/scripts/funnel (§8 OUTSOURCED); this is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house and AI chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this questions→imposition→pull-push→script→guilt-trip framework as the backbone SOP for Palm's future in-house chat team and as AI-chat training material.

consensus_risers.title: Master each channel yourself, then delegate to cheap geo-matched VAs

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can
you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall
you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's posting is partly delegated (Amin manual posting via Telegram §5; editors §1) but staffing/hiring criteria, scorecards and SOP standards are a gap (§10 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a VA hiring + SOP + geo-matching playbook for posting/social roles to fill the §10 staffing gap; applies to current real-creator posting ops.

consensus_risers.title: One reusable script deployed across all subscribers = scalable chat ops

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything
you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script assets. This 'script as reusable product' model is directly applicable to the AI chatting roadmap project — the state machine should implement the script once and deploy it across all fans on a given creator's account.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI chatting project, build the escalation script as a canonical, versioned asset per creator (not per conversation). Track which 'step' in the script each fan has reached using Fan Tracker CRM (§9) so the AI resumes at the correct point. For the in-house chat team, train chatters on script delivery as a skill (making canned feel personal) rather than improv.

consensus_risers.title: Match deal structure to creator tier, not one-size 50/50

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Percentage splits with US/UK/Australian creators outperform paying monthly salaries to lower-cost-region creators because Western audiences spend more and the model carries no upfront cost.
variants.what_they_do: Moved off salaried Colombian/Argentinian models (high overhead, hard to reach US spenders, recurring raise pressure) to zero-upfront percentage deals (e.g. 50/50) with US/UK/AU creators whose audiences actually pay premium PPV prices.

sources.quote: you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K
my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
going from zero to 10K with a salary model is like a billion times harder than with a percentage model from the west
If it's a 50/50, it's pretty much based on what you make her. She takes half

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets a single per-creator commission % at onboarding with no documented tier/retainer/upfront model (§7 THIN GAP — the #1 unwritten gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a tiered Palm pricing model (upfront setup fee for ad-acquired creators; retainer + upside split for established creators) instead of a flat commission, to protect margins and de-risk acquisition spend.

consensus_risers.title: Build backend capacity ahead of creator pipeline, not reactively after signing

variants.claim: An agency must have more operational infrastructure than its current client load requires at all times, so any inbound creator can be onboarded immediately without dropping balls on existing clients.
variants.what_they_do: The speaker frames this as a readiness posture: 'if I know that any given week there might be a creator that reaches out that I'm really interested in, I need to have more infrastructure than needed for my current client base.' Reactive hiring after signing = delayed onboarding, overwhelmed ops, damaged existing accounts, and lost creator trust. The prescription is to treat hiring/staffing as a proactive capacity investment, not a response to demand.

variants.claim: An unsystemised OF backend (no tracking links, no per-creator content plan, inconsistent handling) will collapse under sudden high sub volume, wasting the traffic wins.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis describes an incident where a creator brought in 3,500 subs overnight and the agency had to pull chatters from other models, scramble chat managers, and operate chaotically. His prescribed fix: implement tracking links per traffic source, standardise how every creator's page is managed (content plan, PPV plan, data review), and ensure every process has an SOP so any new chatter/manager can be slotted in without handholding.

variants.claim: Agency infrastructure — dashboards, chatter management hub, and content distribution channels — must be fully configured before onboarding the first creator so signing can be fast and error-free.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin uses: Notion for the agency dashboard and KPI tracking; Discord as the chatter management server (chatters plugged into one repeatable process as they're hired); Telegram for content distribution. All three are set up in the foundation phase, before any outreach begins.

sources.quote: you actually need to have a backend infrastructure ready to take on more profiles before those profiles even are coming to you
you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way
get your notion dashboard set up, your team management, your Discord hub for chatting, your Telegram for content distribution. Everything needs to be set up before even signing a creator
manage these chatters in Discord from day one. That way when you hire more and more, it's just plugging them into the same process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and opportunistic (§6). There's no documented ops-readiness check before signing. The onboarding wizard captures creator info (§6) but does not gate signing on whether Palm has chatter/VA/posting capacity available. As Palm scales, this gap becomes a compounding risk — each new signing without backend capacity degrades existing creator performance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pre-signing ops checklist to Palm's creator onboarding flow: before progressing a prospect to contract, confirm available chatter slots (external team capacity check), Publer/Amin posting bandwidth, and editor queue load. If capacity is at limit, flag for Josh to hire/expand before signing. This is a lightweight systems fix (§systems) that prevents the self-destruction loop the video describes.

consensus_risers.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
consensus_risers.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'
start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

consensus_risers.title: Give high spenders 1:1 personalized treatment, never mass messages

variants.claim: Retain top spenders by excluding them from mass blasts and giving escalating personalized touches as they cross spend thresholds.
variants.what_they_do: Tier fans by spend on a smart list; anyone over a threshold (e.g. $1,500) gets zero mass messages/PPVs, only personalized 1:1. Touches escalate: >$500 first night = personalized voice note (real or ElevenLabs AI); always call them by their saved nickname; >$2,500/mo = a short safe-for-work selfie video saying their name; occasional physical-prize raffles. One client made $56,500 in 30 days from her top 5 fans.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: for big spending fans. You don't send them mass messages or mass pay-per-views... you want to give them that one-on-one personal interaction.
Anybody over $500 in their first night should get personalized voice notes... we can use AI voice notes... 11 Labs.
If the fan has spent more than $2.5,000 in one month, record a short personalized safer work selfie video saying his name.
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale tooling (Fan Tracker, going-cold detection, smart-list-style data) but no written whale-nurture playbook (§9 PARTIAL/GAP); chatting is outsourced (§8), so the tactics are build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Turn whale data into an operating playbook: spend-threshold tiers, exclude-from-mass rule, nickname use, voice-note/selfie-video escalations (AI voice where applicable), raffles — build-toward for in-house/AI chat.

consensus_risers.title: Three-platform funnel stack: IG → TikTok → Threads, each with distinct path

variants.claim: Instagram, TikTok, and Threads each require a different funnel path to convert views into paying fans, and together they cover the highest-ROI free traffic sources for AI models.
variants.what_they_do: IG funnel: views → tagged slave account in bio → link in bio → Fanvue → chat → sale. TikTok funnel: views → IG handle in bio (no adult links allowed) → IG bio link → Fanvue. Threads funnel: views → Telegram link in bio → Telegram DM → Fanvue. Each platform's content format and policy dictates the redirect chain.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: we're only going to go over Instagram, Tik Tok, and threads because all the money I've made so far has been through these three
Funnel one, Instagram. the path they follow, views, tagged account in bio, link in bio, fan view, chatting, and sales
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is not documented (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm uses Publer + Telegram routing for posting but no written multi-platform funnel architecture exists. Palm's real-creator social pipeline (§1) covers IG/TikTok content production but not the cross-platform redirect logic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document explicit per-platform funnel paths for both real and AI creators. For real creators, IG bio → Linktree/Beacons → OF is likely already in use informally; codify it. For the AI persona line (§3), adopt the TikTok→IG→OF redirect chain since TikTok blocks adult links directly.

consensus_risers.topic: Platform-native content formats for AI model social
consensus_risers.title: Format-match by platform: long-caption storytelling on IG, slideshows on TikTok, text posts on Threads

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

sources.quote: Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides
Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is strong at inspo-scraping and replicating viral Reels but is built around video for IG. The Airtable pipeline does not appear to have a Threads text-post workflow or TikTok slideshow format lane. Content format strategy is not documented per-platform.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's content pipeline (§1) to include platform-specific format lanes: (1) TikTok slideshow template workflow, (2) Threads text-post templates. For real creators, these formats can supplement the existing Reels-first approach. For AI personas, add ChatGPT-assisted long-caption storytelling as a content type in the `Inspiration`/`Assets` pipeline.

consensus_risers.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

consensus_risers.title: Promote top-20% chatters to shift leads who own whale accounts and implement systems first

variants.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
variants.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales
every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter structure exists at Palm (§8). This shift-lead model is a concrete org design to adopt when building the in-house team — it creates a promotion path without pulling best chatters away from revenue-generating work.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design the in-house chat org with two tiers from the start: chatters and shift leads. consensus_risers.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters

Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

new_evidence.title: Tag every fan by relationship type and price accordingly

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.

variants.claim: Low/non-spenders need fast, transactional, no-brainer openings, while high spenders need slower, personalized, emotionally invested approaches that reflect the higher ROI of putting time into them.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct opening playbooks: (1) Low/non-spenders (<$50 max purchase): 'Two choices' illusion (free thing + heavily discounted PPV framed as a favor); 'Question game' (ask 2–3 preference questions about color/toy/angle, disappear briefly, return with a PPV 'chosen based on their answers'). Goal is just to get the first purchase and enter the loop. (2) High spenders (>$120 max purchase): 'Opinion opening' (frame a PPV as something the creator is nervous/unsure about, ask for their trusted opinion — if they object to price, say you're 'impartial' and it makes them more curious); 'Personalized PPV' for lapsed high spenders (manually send a high-quality video with a caption referencing their past purchases, their interests from fan notes, priced $150–200, with fan's name in the first 5 words of the last message sent).

variants.claim: Adding emoji tags (whale emoji, feet emoji, etc.) directly to a fan's OnlyFans display name lets chatters instantly identify spend tier and kink preference without opening notes, critical when managing high inbox volume.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters edit the fan's name field in the OF inbox to prepend relevant emojis: 🐳 for whale, specific kink emojis (e.g., foot emoji for feet fetish). This means the chatter sees the tag the moment the chat appears in the list and can tailor the opening message without reading back through history.

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.

variants.claim: Systematically qualifying subscribers on four data points — profession, location, age, and hobbies — lets chatters estimate spending capacity and tailor their communication style before attempting to sell.
variants.what_they_do: After the personalized opener gets a response, chatters move into a structured qualification sequence. (4) Hobbies — reveals personality and gives commonality-building material. Chatters are also taught 'level two qualification' — drilling one level deeper on each topic (e.g., 'How did you get into that job?') to extract more information and signal genuine interest.

new_evidence.title: Track chat performance weekly as a core delivery KPI — don't discover underperformance after revenue drops

He contrasts reactive delivery (everyone panics after revenue drops) with proactive delivery (problem spotted before it becomes painful via data and tracking).

Hire VA chatters at $3–$5/hour + commission once agency hits $2–5K/month. Manage chatters via Discord. Welcome message framework: thank sub, ask their name, build rapport → send teasers → sell PPVs → upsell. Chatting is treated as a strategy, not ad-hoc.

variants.claim: Chatting performance should be measured as a ratio of message/tip revenue to subscription revenue, with elite teams targeting roughly 1:9 to 1:10.
variants.what_they_do: Calculates (messages + tips) / subscriptions; reports turning a ~1:1 ratio creator into 1:10+, and uses the ratio gap on creator calls to show lost revenue (e.g. a 1:3 vs 1:9 difference of thousands/month).

new_evidence.title: Vertical vs. horizontal scale: choose model before building ops

variants.claim: OFM agencies must consciously choose between vertical scale (few creators, salary model, high margin, high risk) and horizontal scale (many creators, rev-share, lower margin, lower risk) because the ops, team size, and marketing obligations are fundamentally different.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter maps two distinct agency architectures: vertical = 1–few salary creators, agency keeps ~90% of revenue, agency owns all marketing; horizontal = many rev-share creators who self-market with agency-provided SOPs, agency takes ~30% cut plus upsell fees. He recommends starting horizontal, then using that cash flow to fund a vertical page for higher margins.

variants.claim: Trans and fetish-adjacent niches are structurally easier to scale to $100K/month because fans are content-driven rather than parasocial-relationship-driven, making cold-traffic channels like GGs and media buying viable without a warm-up funnel.
variants.what_they_do: Agency deliberately targets fetish-driven niches where the fan–creator relationship is transactional. In these niches, cold traffic from meme pages, GGs, and S4S converts directly to paying subscribers without needing a Cupid-style warm-up sequence required for mainstream (cis) creators. This structural difference means the traffic stack is simpler, cheaper, and more scalable. The operator cites knowing multiple agencies doing million-dollar profit months exclusively via trans agencies.

sources.quote: There are two ways that you can scale an OFM company. Okay? You can either vertically scale.
I would take the income that I'm making from that operation and then start injecting it into vertical scale models on a vertical scale page and have the best of both worlds.
I know a couple guys in the OFM space that are doing million-dollar months — they're doing it with a trans agency
in the trans community, people that are into this type of stuff don't care about the creator — they just want to get their hands on as much as they possibly can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently operates as a hybrid leaning vertical — real creators with a revenue-split model, Palm owning social/content and outsourcing chat. Palm's commission structure is per-creator at onboarding (§7) but no documented strategic rationale for vertical vs. horizontal positioning. No explicit decision framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's explicit strategic choice between vertical and horizontal, or define a hybrid model. If Palm wants to scale to many creators, formalize the horizontal SOP-provision model (optimize what creators already have). If Palm stays vertical with fewer high-value creators, document the salary-vs-split decision criteria. At minimum, use this framework in founder strategy sessions.

_new_sources.quote: I know a couple guys in the OFM space that are doing million-dollar months — they're doing it with a trans agency

new_evidence.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

variants.claim: Operators stress that earnings come from marketing, niching and sales rather than from having the most photorealistic model, and a sub-$100 toolchain is enough to compete.
variants.what_they_do: An 'intermediate' workflow (base model + reference set + ChatGPT-generated prompt bank + skin-enhancer for pores/flaws, under $100) is presented as the banker that made five figures; advanced ComfyUI/outsourced-developer models are optional for those with cash, but 'it isn't the quality of your model, it's the marketing, the sales.'

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy
this whole workflow you can set up for under $100
It isn't the quality of your model. It's the marketing, the sales

palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

new_evidence.topic: Organic social as primary agency scaling lever
new_evidence.title: Scale organic social ops as the #1 agency growth lever in 2026

variants.claim: The largest OFM agencies earning hundreds of millions annually built their scale on excellent organic social content years ago, and the rest of the industry must catch up now.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant frames organic social content production — not chatting infrastructure or paid acquisition — as the defining competency separating top-tier agencies from the rest, calling for agencies to laser-focus on producing excellent creative and scaling their social media operations.

variants.claim: Chatting has been commoditized to revshare deals as low as 5%, so agencies can no longer differentiate on chat quality and must compete on organic social marketing instead.
variants.what_they_do: This consultant advises agencies to deprioritize chatting as a differentiator and instead invest heavily in organic social content production as the primary value proposition to creators — arguing that front-end traffic generation is now the only defensible competitive moat.

in 2026 and beyond, the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end

palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm should codify the real-creator posting cadence (frequency, platform-specific timing) to complete the §1 system. new_evidence.title: Done beats perfect; minimize record-to-publish friction

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

new_evidence.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts
they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

new_evidence.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

variants.claim: New chatters should follow a structured three-phase ramp: shadow existing chatters, then practice under live supervision, then work independently under KPI monitoring — rather than going live solo immediately.
variants.what_they_do: Phase 1 (first ~week): new chatter shadows and observes existing chatters in real shifts. Phase 2: chatter handles 2–3 hours of live chat themselves while a supervisor watches in real time. Phase 3: chatter works independently while a supervisor continues to monitor shifts, gives feedback, and answers questions. KPIs and quality controls are only introduced once the chatter reaches a stable independent baseline.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster

let them watch other people, let them supervise other chatters for a couple of days for the first week. Then let them do their initial practice, let them chat for like two or three hours themselves with a supervisor watching.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. new_evidence.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.

variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page
